utterances: Meyer said that this was the least excited she's ever been about casting a vote in a Nevada election.
One piece of good news for Gibbons this week was the release of security footage from the parking garage in question.
Initial reports were that neither he nor Mazzeo appear on the tapes.
Unfortunately for Gibbons, the tapes probably don't have much value, legally speaking, because like so many things in this race, there's no way to know if they're authentic.
Luke Burbank, NPR News, Las Vegas.
I'm reminded of 1960, when Nikita Khrushchev stormed out of the Paris summit after a U2 spy plane had been shot down over the Soviet Union.
Chilly relations with the United States remained on hold, awaiting President Eisenhower's successor, who turned out to be John Kennedy.
NPR's senior news analyst Daniel Schorr.
On his current European trip, his last in office, President Bush is seeking tougher sanctions against Iran for its nuclear programs.
But European leaders appear to be treating him as being on a somewhat sentimental journey.
Iran also acts as though it's awaiting President Bush's successor.
Meanwhile, it's keeping the pressure on and shoring up its influence in Iraq as well as Lebanon and Syria.
Iraqi Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki has spent three days in Teheran, presumably asking the Iranians to back off and allow Iraq to be stabilized.
Maliki is caught in a squeeze between Teheran and Washington.
The Bush administration has been trying to negotiate a status-of-forces agreement legalizing the presence of U.S.
troops after the United Nations mandate runs out at year's end.
Prime Minister Maliki is quoted as saying he would not allow his country to become a platform for harming the security of Iran and other neighbors.
Maliki has also expressed reservations about the status-of-forces agreement, especially the provision that will give American soldiers and contractors immunity from trial in Iraq.
In light of incidents involving shootings of Iraqi civilians, immunity from prosecution is a hard sell for the Iraqi people.
A State Department spokesman says Iraq is a sovereign country that's going to act in its own interests.
He was referring to pressures from Iran, but that could be applied to American pressures as well.
In our presidential campaign, candidates have been vying with each other in toughness on Iran.
But soundbites before a pro-Israeli lobby give no real clue to what will happen come next January.
Until then, the U.S.
and Iran will be testing each other's mettle and, with good luck, avoiding an armed confrontation.
This is Daniel Schorr.
Even before Hurricane Katrina, New Orleans' public school system was in crisis.
Chronic teacher shortages, crumbling schools, a lack of supplies contributed to some of the worst student test scores in this country.
Things were so bad that in July, Louisiana officials handed operations to a crisis management firm, Alvarez & Marsal.

Company executive Bill Roberti became the school district's chief restructuring officer, and now his task is even bigger than he could have imagined: rebuilding the entire New Orleans school system from the ground up.
He joins us now from our studios in New York.
Welcome to you.
Good morning.
So when you've been in the city of New Orleans inside the schools, what have you seen?
(Soundbite of archived interview)
Walter Reed Army Medical Center is the Army's top medical facility, a renowned hospital that serves the wounded from Iraq and Afghanistan.
Beginning last month, a series of articles in the Washington Post describe parts of that sprawling facility in appalling terms: filled with black mold, mouse excrement and broken-down walls.

The reports went on to uncover a frustrating maze of red tape that left soldiers and their families stranded amid the bureaucracy, and a system that was both overwhelmed and, at times, indifferent to their problems.
Last Thursday, Secretary of Defense Robert Gates fired the commander at Walter Reed, Major General George Weightman.
secretary of the Army, Francis J.
Harvey, resigned the following day.
Testimony before a House subcommittee that met at Walter Reed yesterday was emotional and angry.
Annette McLeod testified on behalf of her husband Wendell, a National Guardsman who was wounded in Iraq in 2004.
He suffered brain damage but the army medical board called it a preexisting condition.
Mr.
Harvey made a statement the other day that really bothers me.
He says that he hoped the Washington Post was satisfied because they ruined careers.
First, let me come on record by saying I don't care about your careers.
And we want to hear from you, especially those of you who are a part of the military medical system as patients, doctors or nurses.
Is the care what it should be?
Are you satisfied with the investigation?
Our number is 800-989-8255.
That's 800-989-TALK.
The e-mail address is talk@npr.org.
You can also comment at our blog, npr.org/blogofthenation.
Joining us now is Washington Post reporter Anne Hull.
She, along with Dana Priest, who will join us shortly, broke this story.
Nice to have you on the program, and congratulations.
Well thank you.
It's good to be here.
Did you think - was there - just to clarify from that cut of tape, your purpose when you started out in this story was not to destroy careers, was it?
Of course not, and we were most gratified yesterday watching the hearing to see Dan Shannon and Jeremy Duncan and Annette McLeod speak so eloquently and with such a purity about their situations.
These were the soldiers and the family members who testified.
Yes, yesterday at the field hearings at Walter Reed we had three of the folks we wrote about testifying to a House subcommittee.
And they told their stories in a beautifully eloquent, powerful way.
And more importantly, each said that they had complained to the chain of command several times and no one had listened.
And then to see them there with microphones in front of, you know, members of Congress was just amazing.
How did you get tipped off to this story?
How did the investigation begin?
It began very simply.
A call came in, and Dana actually received a call.
She had lunch with someone, and then I joined the story after that lunch and we began to figure out how we were going to do the reporting.
And we operated mostly separately and spent four months, you know, talking to as many people as we could, largely inside the gates of Walter Reed.
And getting inside those gates is not necessarily easy if you identify yourself as a reporter.
Well I - you know, when the war first started, I spent two weeks on Ward 57, which is the amputee ward.
And I was sort of doing around-the-clock what's it like on the amputee ward at the start of the war.
And, you know, Walter Reed was more easy to deal with then.
As the wars progressed four years later, they are very stingy about what they let you see.
So we decided to go remove the filter, as it were, and just sort of freelance.
What do you mean by freelance?
Obviously, you're working for the Post.
Sure.
I mean, we didn't go through the Army for permission, nor did we go through Walter Reed.

We went to the soldiers, removing that middle filter because we wanted to hear what their lives were like and we wanted to witness these problems firsthand, and that required lots of time with these people as they went through their days.
Much of that time and much of the attention has focused on what you found in a facility known as Building 18.
First of all, tell us what it is and tell us what you saw there.
Building 18 is a former motor lodge across the street from Walter Reed.
It's actually outside the post.
And it's a fairly dank, old motel, and since the war in Iraq has been used to house wounded soldiers.
And it is - many rooms had mold.
There were mice - there was a mice infestation in the building, roaches.
One room, you know, you could go into the bathroom and see the next guy's tub above you.
The floor was rotted out.
And it was not a good place to live for anyone, let alone wounded soldiers.

And there were many psychiatric patients living in that building who have PTSD - post traumatic stress - and to live in that sort of environment, and outside the protective gates of Walter Reed, was very stressful and actually hindered the recovery of a lot of the people we talked to inside that building.
Yeah, I remember reading about the solder with open wounds, open sores still from surgery, in a room with black mold.
That's right and, you know, that's just not acceptable.
Now Building 18 was absolutely the worst of the living facilities or barracks at Walter Reed.
The other place we focused on was the opposite end of the spectrum.
It's a very nice hotel called the Malone House.
And the Malone House is like a Holiday Inn Express in the rooms, but the foyer is quite grand and it's a Georgian revival-style hotel.
And prior to the wars in Afghanistan and Iraq it was used to house family members of, you know, convalescing two-star generals who were getting hip replacement surgery at the hospital.
But it has been completely overtaken in the last four years by the wounded and it's really a shock to walk into the Malone House for the first time.
Because you have young people missing arms, missing legs, missing eyes, faces destroyed, parts of their - they're missing skullcaps from surgeries.
It's just a surreal, heartbreaking experience.
And there are also then systemic problems, not just these individual buildings.
The system, for one thing, seemed to be overwhelmed - did not plan for this number of casualties.
Anyway, we're talking with Dana Priest and Anne Hull, the Washington Post reporters who uncovered the scandal at Walter Reed Army Medical Center.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
And let's bring another caller on the line.
This is Quentin(ph).
Quentin is with us from Chicago.
Right, or the fact that it went on so long.
I mean, the simplest things fell through the cracks every day for these wounded soldiers.
And you have to remember they're not healthy.
They are highly medicated.
They are in pain.

They are trying to adjust to their new lives, often missing limbs or other problems and, you know, they're being - one guy we spoke to, Dan Shannon, had to bring in his purple heart to prove he even served in Iraq, and he's a sniper who was shot in the head and is missing an eye.
Doctors' appointments are delayed, or lost, or dropped off the computer.
Medical records are mixed up.
Caseworkers were in severe shortage.
And there are also some civilian workers at Walter Reed who sort of, in the minds of the soldiers, weren't paying them the respect that they deserved.
One soldier showed up at an ophthalmology clinic at five minutes before 7:00 am and he was missing an eye.
And the clerk said, we're not open for five minutes.
Can you come back?
And this is a person who spent a year in a desert serving his country.
And those small injuries day after day really add up and make up for one heck of a frustrating situation.
And then there is the even more frustrating situation of bureaucratic red tape.

Not just missed appointments and that sort of thing, but not knowing where you're supposed to go, not knowing where you are in the system, especially for those trying to transition out of the Army into veterans affairs hospitals.
That's right.
The great sort of black hole is the disability process in which the Army must decide whether a soldier can return to duty or has to be medically retired.
This is a very lengthy process that involves teams of doctors, and these reports take time.
And keep in mind, Walter Reed is a teaching hospital.
On top of that you have people being deployed.
So doctors are constantly leaving, and this creates more of a problem for some of the wounded trying to get appointments.
And its evaluation process for their disability rating can take as long as a year, and the soldiers are deathly afraid of getting low-balled on their disability rating.
And this anxiety about the Army not paying them for retirement is palpable when you walk around Walter Reed.
Let's get some listeners in on the conversation.
We're speaking with Anne Hull.
In just a moment Dana Priest, also of the Washington Post, will join us.
If you'd like to be part of the conversation, 800-989-TALK.
E-mail us, talk@npr.org.
Let's begin with Rick(ph).
And Rick is with us from Ogden, Utah.
How you doing?
Well, thank you.
Yeah, I just wanted to tell you about some of the experiences that I had when I was in there.
You really didn't depend on them for any real medical attention.
I mean, anybody that I knew of that was in there would go to private practice.
I was in a severe car wreck and there was virtually no therapy for me.
They didn't check me out.
I passed out four times before I even made it to the hospital and - whew, I'm already tired.
And it's just ridiculous.
They - let me catch my breath.
Calm down just a little bit, yeah.
Yes, it's quite exasperating for me every time I think of it.
I was in back in the '70s, and we had people dying in the barracks, especially if were a basic or (unintelligible) and they'd just send you back to the barracks.
Spinal meningitis was rampant, and we were expendable.
And that's pretty much the bottom line.
We are expendable.
Even when we're injured and go to the hospital, even stateside, we're still considered expendable.
I wonder, Anne Hull, the anger and the resentment that you can hear in Rick's voice, I assume that's familiar to you.
It's quite familiar and we heard that over and over.
And, you know, another common refrain was once a soldier is injured, he or she becomes a second-class citizen in the eyes of the Army.
They've lost some importance and they feel they are neglected once they make that transition from soldier to patient.
Rick, I hope things are doing better for you now.
Thank you.
